---
title: "Assignment II: Group [1]"
author: "Zhang chuxi"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true # <= check here: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/formats/html.html
execute:
  echo: true    # <= knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE) in Rmarkdown
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Name

[**Name**]{style="color:red"}**: Zhang chuxi**

# Generative AI declaration

Students may use generative AI tools during assignment, for example, to check and improve code, provided that the content is original and reflects the student's own contributions. If generative AI is used, students must declare its use in the box below. An example statement is: “*During the assignment, I used \[XXXX\] to \[XXXX\]. I subsequently reviewed and edited the code and take(s) full responsibility for the final version of the code.”*

::: callout
*During the assignment, I used deepseek to help me understand the requirment and teach me the code about making* Density heatmap animation more beautiful*. I subsequently reviewed and edited the code and take(s) full responsibility for the final version of the code.*
:::

# Dataset

Each group member may choose either of the following dataset:

**Built-in dataset**

-   `palmerpenguins::penguins`
-   `ggplot2::msleep`
-   `ggplot2::midwest`
-   `datasets::quakes`

**Public dataset**

BIOTIME (CC0) <https://biotime.st-andrews.ac.uk/usageGuidelines.php>

-   `BIOTIME_raw_data_192.csv` (uploaded on Canvas) (Whale data from <https://biotime.st-andrews.ac.uk/selectStudy.php?study=192>)
-   `BIOTIME_raw_data_232.csv` (uploaded on Canvas) (Fish data from <https://biotime.st-andrews.ac.uk/selectStudy.php?study=232>)

# Figures by each group member

### Dataset

::: callout-important
[Which dataset did you choose?]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here

`BIOTIME_raw_data_192.csv`
:::

### Introduction

::: callout-important
[What is the dataset about? What variables are included in the data? How was the data taken/measured?]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here

*What is the dataset about?*

It is about different whales' abundance and location,including when and where was it discovered and how many were there.

*What variables are included in the data?*

year,latitude,longitude,abundance,valid_name

*How was the data taken/measured?*

It is from BIOTIME database.
:::

### Interactive plot(s) using `plotly`

::: callout-important
[Please explain which plot type you choose and why. Also, please discuss the strengths and weaknesses of your plot(s).]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here

I chose an interactive faceted scatter plot using ggplot2 and plotly,which clearly reveals how population counts change over time.

At first i would like to use a map,but i found it is too unclear,even my computer go wrong because there are too many points, so i decide to try points.After a lot of thinking, I found that i am curious about when all kinds of whales were discovered. I ask deepseek whether it is deserved to explore.It answer its ok. So i do it.

Then i try to use plotly,but the six different whales in a picture is too unclear and ugly too.so i try to use facet_wrap to show 6 species respectively,which is more clear.The picture is more clear but the year is too a lot makes it too crowded.So i learn from deepseek about angle and words sizes to make it more beautiful.

Then it is successful!!!!!

Strengths

Faceting reduces overlap and makes comparison across species clear. Interactive plot allows zooming, panning, and hovering to view exact values. Transparent points avoid overcrowding and show data density. Clear labels and titles improve readability.

Weaknesses

Too many facets may make the chart look small in some layouts. Hover information only appears in interactive mode, not in static images. Some points still overlap in high-density areas.
:::

::: callout
I also choose heatmap. This is something I started after i finished the aniimation.I found the animation cannot tell us which field has the most whale directly. So this map makes people to know in which sea areas these types of whales are accustomed to showing up,making it easier for people to find them.

Strengths

Clear species distribution, easy to compare.Hotspots obvious, helpful for field search.

Weaknesses

Over-smoothing may blur real positions.Annual changes not obvious in animation.Hard to observe overall distribution.
:::

Note: At least one plot is required, but multiple plots are allowed

::: callout-important
[What is the purpose of this visualization?]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here

About interactive faceted scatter plot

The purpose of this visualization is to show the temporal trend and spatial distribution of whale abundance in Antarctica, compare population differences among species, and help viewers understand how whale counts have changed over decades. It aims to reveal patterns that are difficult to see in a static graph using interactive and faceted plots.

About heatmap

To identify the distribution hotspots of different whale species by longitude and latitude, so as to provide a reference for future whale survey and conservation work.
:::

Note: If you want to specify setting for a code chunk, you can use `#|` for each option (for example, `#| echo: true`). Rmarkdown code chunk still works (for example, `{r echo = true}`), but Quarto style is recommended.

```{r}
#| echo: true
# YOUR CODE HERE
# xxxxx
library(tidyverse)
library(gganimate)
library(plotly)

df <- read_csv("C:/Users/ZhangChuxi/OneDrive - HKUST Connect/桌面/Assignment2/DASC3240_Assignment2/data/BIOTIME_raw_data_192.csv")


g1<- ggplot(df, aes(YEAR, ABUNDANCE, color = valid_name)) +
  geom_point(size=1, alpha=0.2) +
#to make it more beautiful by arrange it 2x3
  facet_wrap(~ valid_name,nrow = 3, ncol = 2) + 
  labs(title="the number of whales in Antarctica varies with the years") +
  theme_minimal()+
#to make it more beautiful by make the words smaller and sideling to avoid crowded
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    strip.text = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.position = "right"
  )
  
p1 <- ggplotly(g1)
p1
```

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(gganimate)
library(plotly)

df <- read_csv("C:/Users/ZhangChuxi/OneDrive - HKUST Connect/桌面/Assignment2/DASC3240_Assignment2/data/BIOTIME_raw_data_192.csv")
#load the map

world_map <- map_data("world")
  
g2<-ggplot() +
#draw the heatmap
  stat_density_2d(
    data = df,
    aes(x = LONGITUDE, y = LATITUDE, fill = after_stat(level)),
    geom = "polygon",
    contour = TRUE,
    n = 500,             
    h = c(2.2, 2.2),       
    bins = 100,          
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
#draw the map
  geom_polygon(data = world_map,
               aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group), 
               fill = "grey90", color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  
# focus on South Pole
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(55, 105), ylim = c(-72, -50)) +
  facet_wrap(~ valid_name, ncol = 3)+
#make it more beautiful
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c(
      "transparent",     
      "#7E7BB9",         
      "#5C6BC0",         
      "#4FC3F7",         
      "#66C2A5",         
      "#FDE725"          
    ),
    name = "Relative density"
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Whale Heatmap ",
    x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#ECEFF4", linewidth = 0.3),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    legend.key.height = unit(1.2, "cm"), 
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8)
  ) 
p2 <- ggplotly(g2)
p2
```

### Animation using `gganimate`

::: callout-important
[Please explain which animation type you choose and why. Also, please discuss the strengths and weaknesses of your animation.]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here

I choose Scatter plot animation and Density heatmap animation.

The animated scatter plot is used to show the exact locations of individual whale observations and how they change over time.It clearly distinguishes different species by color, allowing us to see which species appear where and when, and observe shifts in their spatial distribution year by year.

The kernel density heatmap animation focuses on identifying high‑density hotspots of whale occurrence.It reduces visual clutter from overlapping points and highlights where whales are most concentrated, making overall distribution patterns and changes in aggregation areas much easier to interpret.

Animated Scatter Plot Strengths Clearly shows individual observation locations and allows tracking of spatial shifts over time. Uses color to distinguish different species, supporting species-level comparison. Preserves all original point information without aggregation or distortion. Animation is smooth and easy to follow year by year.

Animated Scatter Plot Weaknesses Points overlap heavily in high-density areas, making patterns hard to see. Too many transparent points can look messy and reduce readability. Does not highlight hotspots effectively.

Kernel Density Heatmap Animation Strengths Effectively shows distribution hotspots and reduces overlap issues. Provides a clear overview of where whales are concentrated. Visual pattern is strong and easy to interpret at a glance. Cleaner and more professional for geographic temporal data.

Kernel Density Heatmap Animation Weaknesses Hides individual locations and exact observation counts. Sensitive to binning and bandwidth choices. Cannot distinguish between different species in one map.
:::

Note: At least one animation is required, but multiple animations are allowed

::: callout-important
[What is the purpose of this visualization?]{style="color:darkred; font-weight:bold;"}
:::

::: callout
### Your answer is here

The scatter plot shows the exact locations of different species year by year, to observe where and when each species appears.

The heatmap shows the overall density and hotspots of whales, making concentration areas and their changes over time much clearer.

Together, they show both detailed species information and overall distribution patterns, providing a complete analysis of spatial-temporal changes.
:::

```{r}

#The scatter plot 
library(tidyverse)
library(gganimate)
library(plotly)
library(maps)

df <- read_csv("C:/Users/ZhangChuxi/OneDrive - HKUST Connect/桌面/Assignment2/DASC3240_Assignment2/data/BIOTIME_raw_data_192.csv")



#load the map
world_map <- map_data("world")

p3 <- ggplot() +
#draw the map
  geom_polygon(data = world_map,
               aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group), 
               fill = "grey90",color="white") +
#draw the point about location information  
  geom_point(data = df, aes(x = LONGITUDE, y = LATITUDE, 
                            color = valid_name ),
             alpha = 0.5,size = 2 ) +
#focus the South Pole,if not do this, the points will be too narrow  
  coord_cartesian( xlim = c(55, 105),ylim = c(-72, -50) ) +

#make it more beautiful    
  labs(
    title = "Distribution Change of Species",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude",
    color = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#make animation
p_anim <- p3 +
  
  labs(title = "Spatial Distribution of Species in Year: {closest_state}" ) +
  
  transition_states(YEAR,transition_length = 1, state_length = 5,
                    wrap = FALSE ) +
  ease_aes("linear")

animate(p_anim,nframes = 400,fps = 20,width = 800,height = 600)



```

```{r}
#The heatmap 
library(tidyverse)
library(gganimate)
library(plotly)
library(maps)

df <- read_csv("C:/Users/ZhangChuxi/OneDrive - HKUST Connect/桌面/Assignment2/DASC3240_Assignment2/data/BIOTIME_raw_data_192.csv")


# load the information
world_map <- map_data("world")

# draw the plot
p_anim2 <- ggplot() +
#draw the heatmap
  stat_density_2d(
    data = df,
    aes(x = LONGITUDE, y = LATITUDE, fill = after_stat(level)),
    geom = "polygon",
    contour = TRUE,
    n = 500,             
    h = c(2.2, 2.2),       
    bins = 100,          
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
#draw the map
  geom_polygon(data = world_map,
               aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group), 
               fill = "grey90", color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  
# focus on South Pole
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(55, 105), ylim = c(-72, -50)) +
#make it more beautiful
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c(
      "transparent",     
      "#7E7BB9",         
      "#5C6BC0",         
      "#4FC3F7",         
      "#66C2A5",         
      "#FDE725"          
    ),
    name = "Relative density"
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Whale Heatmap in Year: {closest_state}",
    x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#ECEFF4", linewidth = 0.3),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    legend.key.height = unit(1.2, "cm"), 
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8)
  ) +
  
#do animation
  transition_states(
    YEAR, transition_length = 2, state_length = 5, wrap = FALSE
  ) +
  ease_aes("cubic-in-out")


animate(p_anim2, nframes = 200,fps = 5,
        width = 800,height = 500,res = 100)
```
